evals.prompt: I need to decide whether to switch our customer support platform from Zendesk to Intercom. We've been on Zendesk for 3 years, have about 2,000 tickets/month, 8 support agents. The main drivers: Intercom is 30% cheaper at our tier, our team says the UI is better, and it has a product tours feature we want. Migration would take about 2 months and $15k in engineering time. We've already done a 2-week trial with Intercom. Can you stress-test this decision?
evals.expected_output: Identifies this as a two-way door decision, runs lighter analysis, surfaces key assumptions (cost projection accuracy, migration disruption risk, data migration completeness), finds competitive context on Zendesk vs Intercom, flags blind spots like team retraining costs and customer-facing impact during migration.

assertions.text: The report correctly identifies this as a two-way door (reversible) decision
The report applies lighter/faster analysis appropriate for a two-way door, rather than maximum depth
The report mentions migration risk, data loss, or disruption to support operations during transition
The report identifies hidden costs beyond the stated $15k (e.g., retraining, productivity dip, integration rebuilding)
The report includes external market context about Zendesk vs Intercom based on research (not just restating user's input)

evals.prompt: We're a B2B SaaS company, 3 years old, $2M ARR. I'm deciding whether to shut down our self-serve free tier (currently ~8,000 free users, ~200 convert to paid each quarter) and go sales-assisted only. Rationale: free tier costs us ~$40k/month in infrastructure, our ACV has gone up to $18k so the self-serve funnel feels misaligned. Our head of sales thinks we're losing enterprise deals because we look like a 'prosumer' tool. Board meeting in 3 weeks. Please do a full adversarial review.
evals.expected_output: Identifies this as a one-way door (killing a free tier damages trust, hard to reverse). Full deep analysis. Surfaces assumptions about enterprise perception, calculates the real cost of losing 200 conversions/quarter at $18k ACV vs $40k infra cost, flags PLG signal being lost, counterargument that self-serve is a moat not a liability.

assertions.text: The report correctly identifies this as a one-way door (hard to reverse) decision — killing a free tier damages user trust and is difficult to undo
The report does the math: 200 conversions/quarter × $18k ACV = $3.6M ARR pipeline from free tier, comparing it to the $40k/month ($480k/year) infra cost
The report challenges the assumption that the free tier is causing enterprise deal losses — asking for evidence beyond the sales head's opinion
The report mentions product-led growth (PLG), word-of-mouth, or community value being lost by removing the free tier
The report includes a substantive counterargument against shutting down the free tier (not just a token mention)

evals.prompt: My team wants to expand into the European market. We're a US-based SaaS, $5M ARR, 22 employees. The plan: hire 2 EU-based AEs in Q2, localize the product (3 languages), get GDPR-compliant, set up an EU data center, launch a .eu site, and start outbound into UK/Germany/France by Q3. Total budget ask: $800k over 12 months. I have 18 months of runway. Please do an adversarial review including whether this plan makes sense as structured.
evals.expected_output: Identifies mixed door type (hiring is reversible, GDPR/data center less so). Full analysis. Think Big Work Small section should flag that GDPR + data center + hiring + localization + outbound all simultaneously is very high risk. Recommends sequencing: validate demand first with a few inbound EU customers before building infrastructure.

assertions.text: The report identifies this as a mixed or partially one-way door decision (hiring is reversible; GDPR/data center commitments are harder to undo)
The report includes a 'Think Big, Work Small' section that recommends sequencing the plan to validate assumptions before committing full budget
The report flags the risk of running GDPR compliance, data center setup, hiring, localization, and outbound all in parallel — recommending phased approach
The report flags the runway risk: $800k spend on 18 months runway is a significant portion, and EU expansion often takes longer than planned
The report recommends validating EU demand before building infrastructure (e.g., test with existing inbound, close 2-3 EU deals manually before investing in localization)
